Previous data was obtained from the immunization database (IZ) directly from the Nevada Department of Health and Human Services, while current data is the school survey reports from schools. The data differs as follows: IZ includes all vaccinations given in the state but provides parents with the option to opt out of reporting; any child moving from out of state may not be reported in the IZ data; homeschool data is not included in the school survey data. We updated all data 2022-23 onwards to the school survey data, as currently presented on the Nevada’s public facing school screening dashboard.

Vaccination Type Provided: Up to date measles (met proof of immunity requirement)
Data Source or Link:
New York State Department of Health: 
2019 Onwards: https://health.data.ny.gov/Health/School-Immunization-Survey-Beginning-2019-20-Schoo/btkd-y8bp/about_data
2012 to 2019: https://health.data.ny.gov/Health/School-Immunization-Survey-From-School-Year-2012-2/5pme-xbs5/about_data
Notes:
Enrollment data was compiled across public and non-public enrollment available at https://www.nysed.gov/information-reporting-services/student-enrollment-data. BEDS Day enrollment was utilized to match school enrollment to corresponding school ID in the immunization data. County-level rates were calculated across immunization data file using school-level enrollment data and the corresponding MMR rate. Schools where a match between MMR rate database and school-level enrollment data was not found were dropped from the compilation. For the most recent school year (2024-25), 7.4% of schools were not matched, excluding schools that did not provide MMR rates for the most recent school year.
Please note the following:
•	   This data does not include New York City public schools.
•	   For each BEDS enrollment file, the calendar year was matched to the latter school year (i.e., 2018 was matched to 2017-18, 2019 to 2018-19, etc.) as per the documentation.
•	   If a school was duplicated for a given school year and thus had more than one “Percent Immunized Measles” options, the lower bound was used to ensure a more conservative estimate.
•	   The 2021-22 MMR rate for Seneca county does not include Waterloo Highschool, as the MMR rate documented for Waterloo Highschool 9-12 in 2021-22 was 25%, which contrasts with school years 2017-18 to 2024-25 where the MMR rate was higher than 99.80%. Enrollment was consistent during this time period. Due to Waterloo Highschool’s large enrollment, its inclusion drops the 2021-22 Seneca county MMR rate to 89.64%, a delta of ~10% from 2020-21 rates (98.65%) and 2022-23 rates (98.72%). Further, Waterloo Highschool enrollment is only for grades 9-12, who would have been vaccinated pre-pandemic and thus would not experience pandemic-related disruptions in MMR vaccination. Therefore, Waterloo Highschool was excluded from the Seneca county MMR rate due to this improbable outlier in reported MMR rate.
Major Updates from Previous Iterations: All New York data was updated due to an improved aggregation method based on more comprehensive enrollment data.

Vermont represents any county with a vaccination rate greater than 95% as >95%. Therefore, we cap all vaccination rates at 0.95 for Vermont. The data is reported to the Vermont Department of Health by all public or independent schools.
Schools with less than 10 individuals are not included due to privacy concerns.
Counties with vaccination coverage exceeding 95% are capped and presented as 95%. Schools and kindergartens with <10 students do not have data shown.

Notes: 2-dose MMR County level data not available

Wyoming only provides 2 MMR school survey data for 2022-2023. All other years are obtained from the Wyoming Immunization Registry (WyIR)and not included in our dataset. Furthermore, Wyoming data for other school years is highly variable by age (2018-19 data collects 2 MMR data from 6-year-olds, state average MMR rate is 71%. School years 2019-20, 2020-21, and 2021-22 collect data for 7-year-olds, where the state averages are 72%, 76%, and 76%, respectively). Wyoming reported the data for the calendar year, which we aligned with the upcoming school year (i.e., 2022 data was assigned to 2022-23). Note, the WyIR tends to underestimate true adolescent coverage because actual vaccines administered is often under-reported, as documented here: https://health.wyo.gov/wp-content/uploads/2021/11/WDH-IMM_Immunization-Data-Technical-Notes-Rev..pdf
